## Two kinds of bias

In machine learning the word "bias" has two distinct meanings. **Statistical bias**
is the systematic error that arises when a model is too simple to capture the true
pattern (the "bias" in bias-variance tradeoff). **Societal bias** is the unfair
treatment of certain groups that occurs when a model reflects or amplifies
discriminatory patterns in data or design choices.

This lecture is about the second kind.

## Sources of bias in the ML pipeline

Bias can enter at every stage of a machine learning project:

1. **Data collection.** Historical records may underrepresent certain groups or
   encode past discrimination. A hospital algorithm trained on spending data
   systematically underestimated the health needs of Black patients because
   the healthcare system had historically spent less on their care.
2. **Data preprocessing.** Imputation and encoding choices carry assumptions.
   Filling missing salary values with the mean ignores structural pay gaps.
3. **Feature engineering.** Choosing or weighting features can give dangerous
   influence to proxies for protected attributes.
4. **Data splitting.** A training set that is not representative of the deployment
   population leads to biased performance estimates.
5. **Model training.** Small or imbalanced datasets can cause the model to memorize
   majority-group patterns and ignore minority groups.
6. **Model evaluation.** Aggregate accuracy can hide large disparities across
   subgroups. A fraud detector with 90% overall accuracy may have only 50%
   sensitivity for a specific demographic.

::: {.teacher-only}
The COMPAS case study is effective here. ProPublica showed that COMPAS predicted
Black defendants would reoffend at a much higher false-positive rate than White
defendants, even though overall accuracy was similar.
:::

## Regulation: GDPR and the EU AI Act

The GDPR gives individuals the right to an explanation of automated decisions that
significantly affect them (Article 22). Recital 71 states that individuals can ask
"why" for any such decision and request human intervention.

The EU AI Act classifies AI systems by risk level and imposes transparency,
accountability, and human-oversight requirements on high-risk applications such as
credit scoring, hiring, and law enforcement.

## Explainable AI

If we cannot understand why a model makes a decision, we cannot audit it for
fairness. Explainable AI (XAI) provides tools to open the black box.

**Saliency maps and RISE** highlight which parts of an input (for example, which
pixels in an image) contribute most to the model's prediction. They work without
access to model internals by masking input regions and observing how the output
changes.

**Shapley values** come from cooperative game theory. They assign each feature a
contribution score by averaging its marginal effect across all possible feature
subsets. The computation is exact but exponential in the number of features.

**SHAP** (SHapley Additive exPlanations) is a practical framework that approximates
Shapley values efficiently for tree-based models and deep networks. It can explain
individual predictions and produce summary plots that show which features push the
prediction up or down.

## The accuracy-interpretability tradeoff

More powerful models (deep networks, large ensembles) tend to be harder to interpret.
Simpler models (linear regression, shallow decision trees) are easier to explain but
may sacrifice predictive performance. The right balance depends on the application:
a model that denies someone parole or a loan needs a higher standard of
explainability than a model that recommends a movie.
